text: An endoscopic view reveals a massive diverticulum in the distal esophagus with a wide-based opening. The laparoscopic approach is used for the resection of the epiphrenic diverticulum.

The surgeon ligates the short gastric vessels to free the greater curvature of the stomach, then proceeds to the gastroesophageal junction to identify the left and right crura. Posterior dissection is completed, revealing the epiphrenic diverticulum. The esophagus is dissected proximally, utilizing alveolar tissue planes in the mediastinum. The diverticulum is carefully dissected from its pleural attachments without entering the pleural cavity, and the dissection continues until the proximal edge is identified, allowing the diverticulum to be completely reduced from the chest.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the epiphrenic diverticulum, ensuring full mobilization from the mediastinum. The diverticulum is divided using two fires of a blue load stapler, with reinforced staple lines. A 44 French bougie is placed to prevent esophageal narrowing, and the staple line is inspected before the diverticulum specimen is removed from the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon performs a robotic Heller myotomy using hook cautery, clearing the esophagus to visualize the esophageal wall. The anterior surface of the wall is scored, and the circular muscle layer is identified and divided, working proximally towards the diverticulum. The anterior vagus nerve is preserved as it crosses the esophagus. The myotomy extends onto the stomach for two to three centimeters, reaching the edge of the diverticulum.

The surgeon performs laparoscopic crural closure using an endoscopic suturing device with barbed proline suture. The fundus of the stomach is wrapped posteriorly around the esophagus to initiate the toupee fundoplication. The stomach is grasped near the short gastric vessels, and the shoe shine maneuver is used to ensure the fundus is not twisted, allowing for a loose fundoplication. The suturing device completes the toupee fundoplication, which is then visually displayed.

The completion endoscopy reveals the removal of the diverticulum with an intact staple line and a wide open gastroesophageal junction. The postoperative upper GI imaging is displayed, showing no complications.
